title: LangGraph
oneLiner: A graph framework for stateful AI agent workflows.
summary: LangGraph is a graph-based framework for building stateful agent workflows with durable execution, branching, and human review paths.
whyItMatters: Most serious agent workflows need state, branching, recovery, and review. LangGraph gives builders a more explicit structure than one-off prompt chains.
seoTitle: LangGraph: Open-source framework for stateful AI agents
seoDescription: A concise editorial profile of LangGraph, including what it does, who it is best for, license, repo, and related open-source AI resources.
shareDescription: A graph framework for stateful AI agent workflows.

coreStrengths.title: Explicit graph-based control
coreStrengths.description: LangGraph models an agent workflow as a graph of steps, branches, and transitions instead of hiding orchestration inside one long prompt chain.
coreStrengths.whyItMatters: This makes it easier to reason about complex flows, add checkpoints, and decide where human review or tool execution should happen.

coreStrengths.title: Stateful long-running agents
coreStrengths.description: The framework is built for workflows that need state, persistence, retries, and durable execution across more than one model call.
coreStrengths.whyItMatters: That matters when an agent is doing meaningful work over time, not just answering a single question.

coreStrengths.title: Human-in-the-loop workflow paths
coreStrengths.description: LangGraph supports patterns where a workflow can pause, ask for review, and continue with structured state.
coreStrengths.whyItMatters: Teams can keep control over sensitive or high-impact steps without abandoning agent automation.

useCaseNotes.title: Production agent workflows
useCaseNotes.description: Use it when an agent needs multiple steps, memory of prior state, and predictable control over tool use.

useCaseNotes.title: Research and review loops
useCaseNotes.description: Useful for workflows that gather information, branch into follow-up checks, and let a human approve or redirect progress.

useCaseNotes.title: Internal copilots with state
useCaseNotes.description: A fit for teams building internal assistants that need workflow checkpoints rather than one-off chat responses.

compareNotes.title: Choose it over simple prompt chains when control matters
compareNotes.summary: LangGraph is heavier than a simple chain, but the tradeoff makes sense when you need explicit state, branching, and recovery.

compareNotes.title: Evaluate framework fit before committing
compareNotes.summary: It is best for builders already comfortable owning agent architecture; small one-shot automations may not need this level of structure.
